title: New Lock Installation in Newberry, FL: How to Choose the Right Backset, Bore Hole, and Door Prep
excerpt: Planning a new lock installation in Newberry, FL? Learn how to choose the right backset, bore hole size, and door prep before you buy. Be Secure Locksmith comes to you.
author: Netta Kaiden
metaTitle: New Lock Installation in Newberry, FL: Backset, Bore Hole & Door Prep Explained | Be Secure Locksmith
metaDescription: Planning a new lock installation in Newberry, FL? Learn how to choose the right backset, bore hole size, and door prep before you buy. Be Secure Locksmith comes to you.
body: Three measurements determine whether a lock fits your door correctly: the backset, the bore hole diameter, and the overall door prep. Getting these right the first time saves you from returning hardware, drilling new holes, or scheduling a second visit. Whether you are replacing a worn deadbolt near Newberry’s downtown district or upgrading entry hardware on a rental property off County Road 45A, these specs are the first things we check.
What Is a Backset and Why Does It Matter for New Lock Installation?

The backset is the distance from the edge of your door to the center of the bore hole where the lock cylinder sits. Most residential doors in North Central Florida use one of two standard backset measurements:

If you order a lock with the wrong backset, the latch will not align with the strike plate and the door will not latch or lock properly. Many entry sets now come with an adjustable latch that accommodates both sizes. Before purchasing hardware for a new lock installation in Newberry, FL, measure from the edge of the door to the center of the knob or cylinder hole. That single number will narrow your hardware options and prevent a frustrating mismatch.
What Bore Hole Size Does Your Door Already Have?

The bore hole is the large circular hole drilled through the face of the door that holds the lock body. The standard bore hole diameter for most residential locks is 2-1/8 inches. Older doors sometimes have a smaller bore hole of 1-1/2 or 1-3/4 inches, which means a standard modern lock will not fit without additional drilling. If your bore hole is smaller, we can use a hole saw to enlarge it carefully without splitting or weakening the door. Attempting to force an oversized lock body into a smaller hole can crack the door face or warp the lock mechanism. Our new lock installation service includes a door assessment so the right hardware is confirmed before any work begins.
How Does Door Thickness and Edge Prep Affect Lock Compatibility?

Standard exterior doors are typically 1-3/4 inches thick, while interior doors are often 1-3/8 inches thick. Some heavy-duty deadbolts and smart lock systems are designed specifically for exterior door thickness and will not install cleanly on a thinner door. The edge prep refers to the mortise cutout on the door edge where the latch faceplate sits flush. If your existing cutout is a flat 1-inch by 2-1/4-inch mortise, confirm that your new lock uses the same faceplate shape. A mismatch leaves a gap at the door edge and can affect how smoothly the door closes. Our locksmith service in Newberry covers this compatibility check as part of a standard installation visit.
Does It Matter Whether You Choose a Knobset, Lever, or Deadbolt?
The type of lock hardware you choose affects door prep in a few important ways. Here is a quick breakdown:

• Knobsets and levers require a large bore hole through the door face and a smaller cross-bore through the door edge for the latch
  • Deadbolts need a separate bore hole above the knobset and their own edge cutout for the bolt
  • Smart locks vary by brand and often have unique installation requirements

If you are adding a deadbolt to a door that has never had one, we will drill and prep the door from scratch. Homeowners upgrading to keypad or app-controlled access often benefit from professional installation. You can review what goes into smart lock installation to understand what that process involves for your specific door.
What Should You Check Before Calling a Locksmith for a New Lock Installation in Newberry, FL?
A little preparation before we arrive makes the visit faster and more accurate. Have the following ready:

• Door thickness and whether it swings inward or outward
  • Current backset measurement and bore hole diameter
  • A photo of the existing latch and faceplate on the door edge
  • Any replacement hardware you have already selected, with the box available for reference

For those managing multiple units or rental homes, our property management locksmith services include bulk lock installations and rekeying options that keep all entries consistent. If you want existing locks to work with the same key after adding new hardware, lock rekeying can be done at the same visit.
Get the Right Fit the First Time with Professional New Lock Installation in Newberry

Confirming your backset, bore hole size, and door prep before installation protects your investment and ensures your lock functions correctly from day one. Skipping any one of these checks can result in a lock that sticks, a latch that misses the strike plate, or hardware that will not fit without modifications. We bring the tools and knowledge to assess your door on-site, confirm compatibility, and complete the installation without unnecessary delays.
